Large total stroke volume (forward + regurgitant volume refilling LV)
High SBP (large SV) + low DBP (diastolic runoff into LV through incompetent valve)
LV dilatation from chronic volume overload (eccentric hypertrophy)

clinical_summary.detail: High-pitched, blowing, decrescendo, left sternal border 3rd ICS, sitting forward, end-expiration
clinical_summary.significance: Blood regurgitating from aorta into LV through incompetent valve during diastole
AR jet impinges on anterior mitral leaflet, pushing it partially closed → functional MS → diastolic rumble
Large SV causes visible arterial distension in neck
Wide PP transmitted to capillary bed → visible systolic flushing/diastolic blanching
Abrupt arterial distension from high SV produces audible sound over large arteries

clinical_summary.detail: Systolic and diastolic bruit over femoral with light compression
clinical_summary.significance: THE ONLY peripheral sign directly demonstrating retrograde diastolic flow (regurgitation)

examination_technique.sequence: Pulse character (collapsing test) → BP (wide PP) → Peripheral signs → Precordium → Auscultation (sitting forward, end-expiration at LSB)

actions: COLLAPSING PULSE TEST: Lift patient's arm above heart level. Wrap your fingers around the wrist. In AR, you feel a thudding/slapping pulse against your fingers (rapid rise and rapid fall). Compare with the other arm.
Blood pressure: specifically note the DBP. Very low DBP (<50 mmHg) with wide PP (>80 mmHg) is highly suggestive of severe AR.
Corrigan's sign: look at the neck — visible vigorous carotid pulsation (dance of the carotids).
de Musset's sign: observe patient's head from the side — rhythmic bobbing with each heartbeat.
Quincke's sign: gently press on the nail bed with your thumb to partially blanch it. Release and watch for alternating flushing (systole) and blanching (diastole).
Traube's sign: place stethoscope over femoral artery WITHOUT compression. In AR, you hear a sharp 'pistol shot' sound with each systole.

Duroziez's sign: place stethoscope over femoral artery with LIGHT proximal compression. You hear a systolic bruit (forward flow) AND a diastolic bruit (retrograde flow from AR). This is the only sign that directly demonstrates regurgitant flow.
Hill's sign: measure BP at ankle (popliteal) and arm. Difference >20 mmHg = AR. >60 mmHg = severe AR.
findings_in_this_case: Corrigan's, de Musset's, Quincke's, Traube's, Duroziez's all positive

actions: Position patient SITTING FORWARD with full expiration held. This brings the aortic root closer to the chest wall and maximizes audibility of the EDM.
Listen at LEFT STERNAL BORDER (not aortic area) at 3rd-4th ICS with the DIAPHRAGM (high-pitched murmur).
Character: high-pitched, blowing, decrescendo (loudest at start of diastole, fading as pressure gradient between aorta and LV equalizes). Duration correlates with severity: longer EDM = more severe.
Also listen at apex for Austin Flint murmur: mid-diastolic rumble caused by AR jet hitting the anterior mitral leaflet. Differentiate from true MS: Austin Flint has NO opening snap, NO loud S1.
Check for coexisting AS (ESM at aortic area) — mixed AS+AR is common in RHD and bicuspid valve disease.
findings_in_this_case: EDM at LSB in sitting forward position

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: In diastole, blood regurgitates from the aorta back into the LV through the incompetent aortic valve. This diastolic runoff causes rapid fall in arterial pressure. In systole, the LV ejects a very large stroke volume (forward output + regurgitant volume that refilled the LV) causing rapid rise. The combination produces the characteristic rapid rise + rapid fall waveform.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Large SV in systole → rapid rise] + [Diastolic runoff into LV → rapid fall] → [Collapsing pulse]

findings_with_mechanisms.finding: Wide pulse pressure (high SBP, low DBP)
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: High SBP: LV ejects a very large total stroke volume (forward + regurgitant volumes combined) → arterial system receives a large bolus → SBP elevated. Low DBP: during diastole, blood runs off into LV through the regurgitant valve → aortic pressure falls rapidly → DBP drops. The wider the PP, the more severe the AR.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Large SV] → [High SBP]. [Diastolic runoff] → [Low DBP]. [High SBP - Low DBP] → [Wide PP]
findings_with_mechanisms.grading: PP >80 mmHg suggests severe AR. DBP <50 mmHg suggests severe AR.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: The AR jet strikes the anterior leaflet of the mitral valve during diastole, pushing it partially closed. This creates functional narrowing of the mitral orifice → turbulent diastolic flow from LA to LV → mid-diastolic rumble mimicking MS. Crucially, there is NO opening snap (the mitral valve is structurally normal) and S1 is NOT loud.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [AR jet] → [Hits anterior mitral leaflet] → [Partial closure/restriction] → [Functional MS] → [MDM at apex]
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: KEY EXAM QUESTION: How to differentiate Austin Flint from true MS? (1) No opening snap, (2) No loud S1, (3) Peripheral AR signs present, (4) EDM at LSB present. If examiner asks and you mention opening snap as the differentiator, full marks.

findings_with_mechanisms.finding: Acute vs chronic AR — why peripheral signs are absent in acute
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: In chronic AR, the LV gradually dilates over months-years (eccentric hypertrophy), accommodating the regurgitant volume. Total stroke volume is very large → all peripheral signs develop. In acute AR (IE, dissection), the LV is normal-sized and cannot accommodate the sudden regurgitant volume → LVEDP rises dramatically → LA pressure rises → flash pulmonary edema. Because the LV hasn't had time to dilate, total SV is not increased → no wide PP, no bounding pulse, no peripheral signs. The murmur is short (rapid equalization of aortic and LV pressures). Acute AR is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Acute AR] → [Normal-sized LV] → [Cannot accommodate regurgitant volume] → [↑↑LVEDP] → [Flash pulmonary edema] → [NO peripheral signs]

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: Chronic severe aortic regurgitation of rheumatic etiology with LV dilatation and early decompensation (onset of PND)
supporting_evidence: High-pitched EDM at left sternal border (sitting forward, end-expiration)
Collapsing pulse with wide pulse pressure (160/50, PP = 110)
Multiple peripheral signs: de Musset, Corrigan, Quincke, Traube, Duroziez
Displaced, hyperdynamic apex (LV dilatation from volume overload)
Austin Flint murmur at apex (severe AR impinging on mitral valve)
History of ARF with carditis at age 16
PND onset indicates early decompensation

differentials.why_not: Graham Steell is heard at PULMONARY area (left 2nd ICS), increases with inspiration (right-sided origin). Associated with loud P2, signs of pulmonary HTN, RV failure. NO peripheral AR signs, NO wide pulse pressure. In this patient, multiple peripheral signs confirm AR.

differentials.points_for: Wide pulse pressure, bounding pulse, hyperdynamic circulation
differentials.why_not: No EDM (valve is structurally normal). Look for specific signs: thyrotoxicosis (tremor, exophthalmos, goiter), anemia (pallor, koilonychia). Echo would show normal valve.

differentials.points_for: Can occur together in RHD and bicuspid valve
differentials.why_not: No ESM heard. Pulse is collapsing, not slow-rising (which would be expected if significant AS coexisted). If both present, need to determine which is dominant — affects surgical approach.

investigations.expected: LVH with volume overload pattern (tall R waves in V5-V6, deep Q waves in lateral leads — 'LV volume' pattern). ST-T changes. May show sinus rhythm (AF less common in AR than in mitral lesions — LA pressure is not as severely elevated).
investigations.why: Confirms LV involvement. Sinus rhythm is more common in AR than MS/MR.

investigations.expected: Cardiomegaly with LV configuration (apex displaced laterally and inferiorly — 'boot-shaped' heart). Uncoiled, dilated ascending aorta. No pulmonary congestion until late decompensation.
investigations.why: LV enlargement without pulmonary congestion until late = chronic compensated AR.

investigations.expected: Valve: thickened leaflets (rheumatic), poor coaptation, regurgitant jet. Severity: vena contracta >6mm, regurgitant volume >60ml, holodiastolic flow reversal in descending aorta (severe), pressure half-time <200ms (severe). LV: dilated (LVEDD >65mm), hyperdynamic initially, EF begins to decline at 50-55% (already significant dysfunction in AR). LVESD >50mm = surgical threshold.
investigations.why: Grades severity and determines surgical timing.

investigations.expected: Accurate quantification of regurgitant fraction. Useful when echo is suboptimal.
investigations.why: Gold standard for quantifying regurgitation when echo is equivocal.

medical.rationale: Reduce afterload → increase forward stroke volume → reduce regurgitant fraction
medical.specifics: ACE inhibitors (enalapril, ramipril) or nifedipine. Use in symptomatic patients not yet ready for surgery, or asymptomatic with LV dilatation.

medical.rationale: Slowing HR prolongs diastole → more time for regurgitation → worsens AR hemodynamics
medical.specifics: Exception: if AF develops, rate control is necessary. Use cautiously.
Benzathine penicillin G every 3-4 weeks until age 40 or lifelong with severe disease.

management.interventional: No percutaneous option for AR currently (unlike TAVI for AS). Emerging: transcatheter AR repair devices under investigation.
management.surgical: AVR (aortic valve replacement). Indications: (1) Symptomatic severe AR (any symptoms), (2) Asymptomatic with EF ≤50%, (3) LVEDD ≥70mm or LVESD ≥50mm (progressive dilatation). Mechanical vs bioprosthetic same considerations as AS. Valve-sparing root repair in selected patients (Marfan, annuloaortic ectasia).
management.this_patient: PND onset = decompensation beginning. Needs urgent echo for LV dimensions and EF. If EF ≤50% or LVESD ≥50mm → proceed to surgery. If borderline, optimize medical therapy and reassess in 3 months with serial echo. Restart secondary prophylaxis immediately.
management.follow_up: Serial echo every 6 months for LV dimensions. If LVEDD increasing >5mm/year, consider surgery even if asymptomatic. Post-AVR follow-up as per AS.

complications.mechanism: Chronic volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy → eventually myocyte dysfunction → EF drops → decompensation
complications.presentation: PND, orthopnea, pulmonary edema
complications.management: AVR before irreversible LV damage. EF <50% = already significant damage.

complications.mechanism: Chronic aortic root dilatation (especially in Marfan, bicuspid aortopathy) → weakened aortic wall → dissection
complications.presentation: Tearing chest pain radiating to back, acute severe AR, hypotension
complications.management: Emergency surgical repair. Type A dissection = immediate surgery.

complications.mechanism: Regurgitant jet damages endothelium → nidus for infection
complications.presentation: Fever, new or worsening murmur, embolic phenomena
complications.management: Prolonged IV antibiotics, surgery if vegetations/abscess/hemodynamic instability.

vivas.question: Name the eponymous peripheral signs of AR and their mechanisms.
vivas.answer: All peripheral signs result from WIDE PULSE PRESSURE (high SV → high SBP; diastolic runoff → low DBP). (1) de Musset: head bobbing from carotid pulsation. (2) Corrigan: visible carotid 'dance.' (3) Quincke: nail bed capillary pulsation. (4) Traube: pistol-shot sounds over femoral without compression. (5) Duroziez: systolic AND diastolic bruit over femoral with light compression — the ONLY sign directly demonstrating regurgitant flow. (6) Hill: popliteal SBP >20mmHg above brachial. (7) Mueller: uvular pulsation. (8) Lighthouse: alternating facial flushing/pallor. (9) Landolfi: pupillary pulsation. (10) Rosenbach: hepatic pulsation. (11) Gerhardt: splenic pulsation.

follow_ups.question: Which sign is most specific for AR?
follow_ups.answer: Duroziez's sign — it is the only sign that directly demonstrates diastolic retrograde flow (the diastolic bruit). Other signs are manifestations of wide PP which can also occur in high-output states. Duroziez's confirms that the wide PP is from valvular regurgitation specifically.

vivas.question: How do you differentiate Austin Flint murmur from true MS?
vivas.answer: Austin Flint: NO opening snap (mitral valve is structurally normal), S1 is NOT loud, EDM present at left sternal border (the primary AR murmur), peripheral AR signs present (collapsing pulse, wide PP), apex displaced (LV dilated). True MS: opening snap PRESENT, S1 IS loud, NO EDM at LSB, NO peripheral AR signs, apex NOT displaced (tapping). The OPENING SNAP is the single most reliable differentiator.

vivas.question: Contrast acute and chronic AR.
vivas.answer: Chronic: gradual onset, LV dilates (compensates), large total SV, wide PP, all peripheral signs present, long EDM, symptoms develop over years. Acute (IE, dissection, trauma): sudden onset, LV is normal-sized (no time to dilate), LVEDP rises dramatically, LA pressure rises (premature mitral closure on echo), flash pulmonary edema, NARROW or normal PP, NO peripheral signs, short/soft EDM, cardiogenic shock. Acute AR is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY — mortality without surgery is very high.

follow_ups.question: What echo finding is pathognomonic for acute severe AR?
follow_ups.answer: Premature closure of the mitral valve — the LV pressure rises so rapidly in diastole (from regurgitant volume filling the non-compliant LV) that it exceeds LA pressure BEFORE atrial systole, slamming the mitral valve shut early. This does not occur in chronic AR because the dilated LV accommodates the volume at lower pressures.

vivas.question: What are the surgical indications for AR?
vivas.answer: Class I indications: (1) Symptomatic severe AR (any symptoms — dyspnea, angina, syncope). (2) Asymptomatic with EF ≤50%. (3) Asymptomatic undergoing other cardiac surgery (CABG, ascending aorta). Class IIa: LVEDD ≥70mm or LVESD ≥50mm (progressive dilatation even with preserved EF). (4) Rapid decline in EF or increase in LV dimensions on serial echo.

follow_ups.question: Why is EF threshold 50% in AR (lower than MR's 60%)?
follow_ups.answer: Unlike MR (where LV ejects into low-resistance LA, inflating EF), AR involves ejecting the regurgitant volume back into the aorta (high resistance) in systole. The EF is not as artificially elevated as in MR. Therefore, EF ≤50% in AR represents roughly equivalent myocardial dysfunction to EF ≤60% in MR. Both thresholds aim to intervene before irreversible damage.

presentation_script.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 38-year-old male who presented with an insidious fever of 6 weeks duration, progressive fatigue, weight loss of 5 kg, night sweats, and new-onset joint pains. He has a known history of rheumatic heart disease with mitral regurgitation diagnosed at age 22. He underwent a dental extraction 2 months ago without antibiotic prophylaxis.
presentation_script.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is conscious, febrile (temperature 38.4°C), appears unwell and cachectic. Pulse is regular, 96 per minute, normal volume. Blood pressure is 130/70 mmHg. On examining the hands, I note splinter hemorrhages in two nailbeds and a tender, erythematous nodule on the pad of the left index finger — Osler node. There are non-tender, erythematous macules on the right palm — Janeway lesions. Petechiae are present on the conjunctivae. No clubbing. Splenomegaly is palpable.
presentation_script.system_findings: On cardiovascular examination — apex beat is displaced to the 6th intercostal space in the anterior axillary line, hyperdynamic. There is a pansystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla — consistent with mitral regurgitation — which the patient reports is different from his previously known murmur (new or changed murmur). There is no diastolic murmur.
presentation_script.diagnosis_statement: Subacute infective endocarditis on a background of rheumatic mitral regurgitation, likely precipitated by dental extraction without prophylaxis, with Duke criteria fulfilled — 2 major (predisposing valve + new/changed murmur likely positive, pending blood cultures and echo) plus minor criteria (fever, vascular phenomena: splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions; immunological phenomena: Osler nodes, conjunctival petechiae).
differentials: Systemic lupus erythematosus (fever, joint pains, rash — but Libman-Sacks endocarditis typically asymptomatic, different rash pattern)
Lymphoma (fever, weight loss, night sweats — B symptoms — but no murmur, no embolic phenomena)
Atrial myxoma (constitutional symptoms, embolization — but tumor plop on echo, not vegetations)
Rheumatic fever recurrence (fever, arthritis, carditis — but Jones criteria differ from Duke criteria, migratory arthritis pattern)
tips: ALWAYS mention Duke criteria framework when presenting IE — examiners expect you to classify as definite, probable, or possible.
The triad: fever + new murmur + embolic phenomena = IE until proven otherwise.
Distinguish Osler nodes (PAINFUL, fingertips, IMMUNE complex) from Janeway lesions (PAINLESS, palms/soles, SEPTIC emboli).
Mention the predisposing event (dental extraction without prophylaxis) — this shows clinical reasoning.

case_scenario.chief_complaint: Low-grade fever (6 weeks), fatigue, weight loss (5 kg), night sweats, arthralgias
case_scenario.hpi: Insidious onset of evening fever (38-39°C) with rigors for 6 weeks. Progressive fatigue limiting work. Unintentional weight loss of 5 kg. Drenching night sweats requiring change of bedclothes. Diffuse arthralgias (non-migratory, affects small joints of hands — different from rheumatic migratory polyarthritis). Noticed reddish spots on palms 2 weeks ago. Wife noted pallor. No cough, no dysuria, no skin abscesses.
case_scenario.past_history: RHD diagnosed age 22 — known to have MR. Was advised monthly penicillin — takes irregularly. Dental extraction 2 months ago at local clinic — no antibiotic prophylaxis given. No prior episodes of IE. No IV drug use.
case_scenario.drug_history: Irregular benzathine penicillin. No anticoagulants. No immunosuppressants.

clinical_summary.significance: Continuous bacteremia from valve vegetation seeding blood constantly
Chronic infection → elevated cytokines (TNF, IL-1, IL-6) → catabolism
Microemboli from valve vegetation lodging in nailbed capillaries (vascular phenomenon)

clinical_summary.detail: Tender erythematous nodule on left index fingertip pad
clinical_summary.significance: Immune complex deposition in skin (immunological phenomenon). PAINFUL = immune.

clinical_summary.detail: Non-tender erythematous macules on right palm
clinical_summary.significance: Septic microemboli causing infarction in dermis (vascular phenomenon). PAINLESS = septic.
Microemboli or immune complex deposition in conjunctival vessels
Immune complex deposition + reticuloendothelial activation from chronic bacteremia

clinical_summary.detail: PSM at apex → axilla (MR), changed from previously known
clinical_summary.significance: New or changing murmur = vegetation causing further valve destruction
Pre-existing MR with LV dilatation, now worsening from active valve destruction

examination_technique.sequence: Temperature → Hands (splinters, Osler, Janeway) → Eyes (Roth spots, petechiae) → Mouth (dental health) → Precordium (murmur change) → Abdomen (splenomegaly) → Urine (hematuria) → Neuro (focal signs from cerebral emboli)

actions: Examine ALL nailbeds for splinter hemorrhages (linear, dark red-brown streaks in the distal third of the nailbed). Note: also caused by trauma — look for distribution pattern and clinical context.
Feel fingertip pads for Osler nodes: tender, raised, erythematous lesions. Press gently — patient will report pain. These are IMMUNE complex deposits.
Examine palms for Janeway lesions: non-tender, flat, erythematous macules. These are SEPTIC emboli causing dermal infarction. Painless on pressure.
Clubbing: develops in chronic (weeks-months) IE. Absent in acute IE.
Check for petechiae on hands and forearms.
Eyes: pull down lower eyelid — conjunctival petechiae. Fundoscopy if available: Roth spots (pale-centered retinal hemorrhages from immune complex vasculitis).
Mouth: dental health (source of bacteremia), petechiae on palate.
Abdomen: palpate for splenomegaly (present in 25-60% of subacute IE). Check for renal angle tenderness (renal infarction from emboli).
Neurological: brief screening for focal deficits (stroke from cerebral emboli — occurs in 20-40% of IE).
Urine: dipstick for hematuria (glomerulonephritis from immune complex deposition — immunological criterion).

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Osler nodes: circulating immune complexes (antigen-antibody) deposit in skin of fingertips/toes → complement activation → local vasculitis → painful, raised, erythematous nodules. More common in SUBACUTE IE (time for immune complex formation). Janeway lesions: septic microemboli from vegetation lodge in dermal arterioles of palms/soles → focal necrosis/infarction → painless, flat, erythematous macules. More common in ACUTE IE (S. aureus, large vegetations).
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Immune complexes] → [Fingertip deposition] → [Vasculitis] → [Painful Osler nodes]. [Septic emboli] → [Dermal infarction] → [Painless Janeway lesions]
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Memory aid: OsLer = Larger, Painful (Immune); JAneway = Flat, PAINless (Septic). Or: Osler = Ouch (painful); Janeway = Painless.

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Major criteria (2 points each): (1) Blood cultures: typical organism from 2 separate cultures OR persistently positive cultures. (2) Endocardial involvement: echo showing vegetation, abscess, new dehiscence, OR new valvular regurgitation. Minor criteria (1 point each): (1) Predisposing condition or IV drug use. (2) Fever ≥38°C. (3) Vascular phenomena: arterial emboli, septic pulmonary infarcts, mycotic aneurysm, Janeway lesions, conjunctival hemorrhages. (4) Immunological: Osler nodes, Roth spots, glomerulonephritis, positive rheumatoid factor. (5) Microbiological evidence not meeting major criteria. Definite IE: 2 major, OR 1 major + 3 minor, OR 5 minor.
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Predisposing valve + bacteremia] → [Vegetation] → [Continuous seeding] → [Fever + emboli + immune phenomena]

findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism: Vegetation on the valve causes progressive destruction of leaflet tissue → worsening regurgitation → murmur becomes louder or changes character. New regurgitation through a previously competent valve = new murmur. This is the most specific clinical sign for IE (in conjunction with fever).
findings_with_mechanisms.mechanism_chain: [Vegetation] → [Valve destruction] → [Worsening regurgitation] → [New/changed murmur]
findings_with_mechanisms.caveat: Murmur may be ABSENT in right-sided IE (tricuspid — low-pressure, small vegetations). Up to 15% of IE cases have no murmur at presentation. A new murmur + fever = IE until proven otherwise.

diagnosis_and_differentials.provisional: Subacute infective endocarditis on rheumatic MR, likely viridans streptococcal, precipitated by dental extraction without prophylaxis
supporting_evidence: Fever for 6 weeks (subacute course)
Predisposing: known RHD with MR + dental extraction without prophylaxis
New/changed murmur
Vascular phenomena: splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions
Immunological phenomena: Osler nodes, conjunctival petechiae
Constitutional: weight loss, night sweats, fatigue
Splenomegaly

differentials.points_for: Fever, arthralgia, renal involvement, can have sterile vegetations
differentials.why_not: Libman-Sacks vegetations are typically asymptomatic (small, located on atrial side of mitral valve). SLE has butterfly rash, photosensitivity, serositis, ANA positivity. Joint involvement in SLE is non-erosive polyarthritis. Blood cultures will be sterile in SLE.

differentials.points_for: Fever, weight loss, embolic phenomena, raised ESR
differentials.why_not: Myxoma produces tumor plop (not vegetations on echo), positional murmur variation, no splinter hemorrhages/Osler nodes/Janeway lesions pattern. Blood cultures negative.

RF arthritis is MIGRATORY (large joints), not fixed small joint arthralgias. RF does not cause embolic phenomena (Osler, Janeway, splinters). Jones criteria differ from Duke criteria. ASO/anti-DNase B titers elevated in RF.

investigations.test: Blood cultures (3 sets from 3 different sites)
investigations.expected: Viridans streptococci (most common in subacute native valve IE). Positive from ≥2 sets = major Duke criterion.
investigations.why: MUST be taken BEFORE starting antibiotics. 3 sets 30 min apart from different venipuncture sites.

investigations.expected: Vegetation on mitral valve (oscillating mass on leaflet). TEE sensitivity 90-95% (TTE only 60-75%). May show abscess, leaflet perforation, new MR jet.
investigations.why: Major Duke criterion. TEE is mandatory if TTE negative but clinical suspicion high.

investigations.expected: Normocytic anemia (anemia of chronic disease), leukocytosis (neutrophilia), thrombocytopenia (DIC/splenomegaly)
investigations.why: Baseline + assesses severity of systemic infection.

investigations.expected: Markedly elevated (ESR >50, CRP elevated). ESR is a sensitive but non-specific marker.
investigations.why: Monitors treatment response (should normalize with successful therapy).

investigations.expected: Microscopic hematuria, RBC casts (immune complex glomerulonephritis — minor Duke criterion)
investigations.why: Assesses renal involvement from immune complex deposition.

investigations.expected: Positive in ~50% of subacute IE (non-specific immune activation)
investigations.why: Minor Duke criterion (immunological).

investigations.expected: Cerebral infarct (embolic), mycotic aneurysm, hemorrhage
investigations.why: Cerebral emboli occur in 20-40% of IE. Rule out before anticoagulation.

medical.rationale: High clinical suspicion — start treatment immediately after blood cultures drawn
medical.specifics: Native valve, subacute: IV ampicillin + gentamicin (covers streptococci + enterococci). Adjust based on culture sensitivity. Total duration: 4-6 weeks IV.

medical.rationale: Targeted antibiotic based on organism and sensitivity
medical.specifics: Viridans strep (MIC ≤0.12): IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone × 4 weeks. With gentamicin synergy × 2 weeks shortens to 2-week regimen. Enterococci: ampicillin + gentamicin × 4-6 weeks. S. aureus: IV nafcillin/oxacillin (MSSA) or vancomycin (MRSA) × 6 weeks.
Monitor renal function (gentamicin nephrotoxicity, immune complex GN). Repeat blood cultures at 48-72h to document clearance. Serial echo to monitor vegetation size.

management.surgical: Surgery indicated for: (1) Heart failure from valve destruction (most common indication). (2) Uncontrolled infection (persistent bacteremia >7 days despite appropriate antibiotics, abscess formation). (3) Prevention of embolism (large vegetation >10mm with embolic events, OR >15mm even without events — increasingly recommended). (4) Fungal or resistant organism IE. Surgery = debridement + valve repair or replacement.
management.this_patient: Draw 3 sets of blood cultures immediately, then start empiric IV ampicillin + gentamicin. Urgent TTE (TEE if TTE inconclusive). Monitor for embolic events (daily neuro exam, renal function). If vegetation >10mm or hemodynamic deterioration → early surgical referral. Ensure secondary prophylaxis lifelong after recovery.
management.follow_up: Weekly blood cultures until negative. Serial echo (weekly initially, then 4-6 weeks after completion). Monitor renal function. Dental evaluation and treatment of any dental sources. IE prophylaxis before all future high-risk procedures. Secondary penicillin prophylaxis lifelong.

complications.mechanism: Vegetation fragments detach → travel to cerebral circulation → lodge in cerebral arteries → infarction
complications.presentation: Acute focal neurological deficit (hemiplegia, aphasia, visual loss)
complications.management: CT brain urgently. Anticoagulation controversial (risk of hemorrhagic transformation). Early surgical intervention for vegetation removal.

complications.mechanism: Septic emboli lodge in vasa vasorum of cerebral/systemic arteries → infection weakens arterial wall → aneurysm formation
complications.presentation: Sudden severe headache (SAH from rupture), focal neuro signs
complications.management: CTA/MRA to detect. Neurosurgical/endovascular intervention if ruptured.

complications.mechanism: Progressive leaflet destruction → worsening regurgitation → acute volume overload → pulmonary edema
complications.presentation: Acute dyspnea, pulmonary edema, cardiogenic shock
complications.management: Most common indication for surgery in IE. Urgent valve repair/replacement.

complications.mechanism: Infection extends beyond valve leaflet into surrounding tissue (aortic root, interventricular septum)
complications.presentation: Persistent fever despite antibiotics, new conduction abnormality on ECG (PR prolongation — abscess near AV node)
complications.management: TEE to diagnose. Surgery required — antibiotics alone insufficient for abscess.

complications.mechanism: Circulating immune complexes deposit in glomerular basement membrane → complement activation → inflammation
complications.presentation: Microscopic hematuria, proteinuria, rising creatinine, RBC casts on urine microscopy
complications.management: Treat the IE (eliminate antigen source). Renal function usually recovers with infection control.

vivas.question: Explain the Modified Duke Criteria for diagnosing IE.
vivas.answer: MAJOR criteria: (1) Microbiological: typical organism from 2 separate blood cultures (viridans strep, S. bovis, HACEK, S. aureus, enterococci without primary focus) OR persistently positive cultures (≥2 samples >12h apart, or 3/3, or majority of ≥4 samples). (2) Endocardial involvement: echo showing oscillating vegetation, abscess, new partial dehiscence of prosthetic valve, OR new valvular regurgitation. MINOR criteria: (1) Predisposition (valvular disease, IV drug use). (2) Fever ≥38°C. (3) Vascular: emboli, Janeway, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial hemorrhage. (4) Immunological: Osler, Roth spots, GN, RF positive. (5) Micro not meeting major criteria. DEFINITE: 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor.

follow_ups.question: This patient — how many criteria does he meet?
follow_ups.answer: Major: (1) Predisposing condition + new murmur (pending echo confirmation for vegetation) — likely 1 major (echo). Pending blood cultures for 2nd major. Minor: (1) Predisposing valve, (2) Fever, (3) Vascular: splinter hemorrhages + Janeway lesions, (4) Immunological: Osler nodes + conjunctival petechiae. At minimum 1 major + 4 minor = DEFINITE IE even before blood culture results.

vivas.question: Differentiate Osler nodes from Janeway lesions.
vivas.answer: Osler nodes: PAINFUL, tender, raised, erythematous nodules on FINGERTIPS/TOE PADS. Mechanism: IMMUNE COMPLEX deposition → vasculitis. More common in SUBACUTE IE. Janeway lesions: PAINLESS, flat, erythematous macules on PALMS/SOLES. Mechanism: SEPTIC microemboli → dermal infarction. More common in ACUTE IE (S. aureus). Memory: Osler = Ouch (painful, immune); Janeway = Painless (septic). Both are on the Duke criteria — Osler = immunological criterion, Janeway = vascular criterion.

vivas.question: When is surgery indicated in IE?
vivas.answer: 4 main indications: (1) Heart failure from valve destruction — MOST COMMON indication, don't delay for completion of antibiotics. (2) Uncontrolled infection: persistent fever/bacteremia >7 days despite appropriate antibiotics, OR abscess formation (paravalvular). (3) Prevention of embolism: vegetation >10mm with 1 embolic event, OR vegetation >15mm even without events. (4) Specific organisms: fungal IE, highly resistant organisms (MRSA with poor response). Surgery = debridement of infected tissue + valve repair (preferred) or replacement.

follow_ups.question: Should you complete antibiotics before surgery?
follow_ups.answer: NO — do not delay surgery to complete antibiotic course if there is a clear surgical indication. Continue antibiotics postoperatively to complete the total planned duration (counting from the date of surgery as day 1 if valve cultures are positive, or continuing the pre-operative count if valve cultures are negative).

vivas.question: What organisms cause IE in different clinical settings?
vivas.answer: Native valve — subacute: viridans streptococci (most common), Streptococcus bovis (always colonoscopy to rule out colon cancer). Native valve — acute: Staphylococcus aureus (most common in acute, most common overall in developed countries). IV drug use: S. aureus (typically right-sided — tricuspid valve). Prosthetic valve — early (<60 days): coagulase-negative staphylococci, S. aureus. Prosthetic valve — late (>60 days): viridans strep, enterococci. Culture-negative: HACEK group (Haemophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella) — slow-growing, need prolonged incubation.

follow_ups.question: Why must you do colonoscopy for S. bovis IE?
follow_ups.answer: Streptococcus bovis (now S. gallolyticus) bacteremia/IE has a strong association with colorectal neoplasia (cancer or polyps) — found in up to 25-50% of cases. The organism colonizes the colonic mucosa, particularly near neoplastic lesions (disrupted mucosal barrier allows translocation). Colonoscopy is MANDATORY even if the patient has no GI symptoms.
